name: Brayton Cycle
summary: The thermodynamic backbone of gas-turbine propulsion, describing how compression, combustion, and expansion produce thrust or shaft power.
whenToUseIt: Use it when reasoning about turbojets, turbofans, and turboprops and how pressure ratio, turbine temperature, and bypass change performance.
steps: Define the compressor inlet state and target pressure ratio.
Add heat through combustion to set turbine entry temperature.
Expand through turbine stages to drive the compressor and fan.
Expand the remaining energy through the nozzle or propulsor.
Compare cycle efficiency, specific thrust, and fuel burn across concepts.
example: A designer comparing a regional turboprop with a small turbofan can use the Brayton-cycle lens to understand why the turboprop is more efficient at lower speeds while the turbofan gains an advantage in faster cruise.
